sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, Michelle, injured her right knee while skiing in Vermont last weekend.
She caught her ski while coming off the lift and fell, causing her knee to pop and resulting in ongoing pain.
She was unable to bear weight and was taken down the mountain by the ski patrol.
The pain is located on the outside of the knee, and she feels like it won't hold her weight, with a sensation that it will buckle.
She was able to bend her knee immediately after the injury but not now.
Straightening the knee causes significant pain.
She has no numbness or tingling in her toes and can feel them okay.
She is a busy mom of two four-year-old twins and is otherwise generally healthy, with no high blood pressure, diabetes, or other chronic conditions.
Past medical history: [NOT MENTIONED]
Review of systems: [NOT MENTIONED]
Current medications: I prescribed ibuprofen 600-800mg every 8 hours as needed for pain.
Objective:
Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]
Physical exam: The patient has edema and an effusion over the right knee, with pain to palpation of the right lateral knee.
There is decreased flexion and extension of the right knee.
The Lachman sign is positive.
Palpable dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulses are present, with no leg edema in the ankle.
Test Results: [NOT MENTIONED]
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient likely has a ruptured anterior cruciate ligament (ACL) in her right knee, based on the physical exam and history of a traumatic injury.
Problem list: ACL rupture; status: suspected
Plan:
I ordered a knee MRI to confirm the diagnosis and rule out other potential injuries.
I recommended continued use of ibuprofen for pain management and advised the patient to wear a knee brace for support.
I will follow up with the patient in a few days to discuss the results of the MRI and develop a plan for further treatment.
Follow-up: I will see the patient again soon to discuss the MRI results and determine the next steps for her care.
